courses.title: Marine Biology
courses.instructor: Dr. Laila P. Barada
courses.description: This is an introductory marine biology lecture course exploring biological principles of structure, function and adaptation for marine life. It includes a review of the history of marine biology and a discussion of local species of marine plants and animals and major marine communities. Human interaction and impact on the ocean is also discussed. Prerequisite: TI Biology 17<197>3 Units DS Marine Biology AD Eligibility for ENGL C1000 or qualification by appropriate AD assessment. NT Enrollment in Biology 18 is not required.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: TI Biology 17<197>3 Units DS Marine Biology AD Eligibility for ENGL C1000 or qualification by appropriate AD assessment.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Biology
courses.instructor: Dr. Shawn C. McEachin, Dr. Darcie McClelland, Dr. Eduardo Ayala, Polly A. Parks, Erica Sharar, Sumana Pasala, Ms. Jessica A. Padilla, Karla E. Villatoro, Rebecca J. Hernandez, Dr. Darcie L. McClelland
courses.description: This survey course covers basic principles in multiple biological fields. It includes the study of molecular and cellular biology, biotechnology, ecology and evolution. Organ systems' structure and function are applied throughout the units. Note: Students will not receive UC credit for Biology 10 if taken after Biology 110 or Biology 120. Prerequisite: TI Biology 10<197>4 Units DS Fundamentals of Biology AD Eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Environ Aspects Biology
courses.instructor: Mr. Brandon MacDonald, Ms. Sally Ahad, Karla E. Villatoro, Erica Sharar, Mr. Bryan Carey
courses.description: Basic ecological and biological principles and concepts are emphasized in the study of the structure and function of ecosystems. Major ecological problems such as over-population, resource depletion and food production are related to endangered species and habitat degradation. Environmental pollution of air and water resources are considered in local areas as well as national and international situations. Air quality and global warming issues are considered. Students are encouraged to participate in local activities addressing environmental problems and restoring and improving local habitats. Prerequisite: TI Biology 15<197>3 Units DS Environmental Aspects of Biology AD Eligiblity for English 1A

courses.title: Fundamentals of Zoology
courses.instructor: Mr. Brandon MacDonald
courses.description: This course introduces major animal groups and the single-celled protozoans. Comparative studies of animal structure and physiology will illustrate principles of classification and evolution. Ecological topics include animal behavior, natural history, and relationships of animals to humans. Genetics and basic cell biology are also covered. Prerequisite: TI Biology 11<197>4 Units DS Fundamentals of Zoology AD eligibility for English 1A

sections.notes: This is a fully online class with no scheduled meeting dates. Registered students must login to the Canvas site (http://www.elcamino.edu/library/distance-ed/Canvas_Students.asp) on the first day of the semester and follow the instructions or you may be dropped from the class.

courses.title: Qual and Reg Compliance in the Biosci
courses.instructor: Dr. Eduardo Ayala
courses.description: This course will cover quality assurance and regulatory compliance for the bioscience industries. Topics will span quality control and Food Drug Administration (FDA) regulations for the biotechnology, biopharmaceutical, biomedical device, and food industries. Theories and application of quality assurance and quality control will be presented and several different quality systems will be discussed such as GMP (good manufacturing practices), ISO9000 (International Standards Organization), Six Sigma, and Lean. Prerequisite: TI Biology 78<197>2 Units DS Quality and Regulatory Compliance in the Biosciences

courses.title: Environmental Science I
courses.instructor: Dr. T. James Noyes
courses.description: This course is a multidisciplinary introduction to environmental and natural resource issues, with an emphasis on how they can be understood in terms of physics, chemistry, and biology. This course examines human population growth through history, resources, pollution, and sustainability, and relates them to how humans use and affect the hydrosphere, atmosphere, lithosphere, and biosphere. Prerequisite: TI Geology 7<197>3 Units DS Environmental Science I AD Eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Fire Behavior/Combustion
courses.instructor: Chen J. Suen, Edward S. Valdez, Daniel J. Conroy
courses.description: This course examines the theory and fundamentals of how and why fires start, spread, and are controlled, as well as an in-depth study of fire chemistry and physics, fire characteristics of materials, extinguishing agents and fire control techniques. Prerequisite: TI Fire and Emergency Technology 5<197>3 Units DS Fire Behavior and Combustion AD Fire and Emergency Technology 1; eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Fire Protect Organization
courses.instructor: Ryan E. Carey, Daniel J. Conroy, Edward S. Valdez, Mr. Joshua A. Boies, Matthew P. Hallock
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to fire protection. Emphasis is placed on career opportunities in fire protection and related fields and the philosophy and history of fire protection. Additional topics include fire loss analysis; organization and function of public and private fire protection services; fire departments as part of local government; laws and regulations affecting the fire service; fire service; specific fire protection functions; basic fire chemistry and physics; introduction to fire protection systems; and introduction to fire strategy and tactics. Prerequisite: TI Fire and Emergency Technology 1<197>3 Units DS Fire Protection Organization AD eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Introduction to Nutrition
courses.instructor: Myra Bremen, Jill K. Gray, Brooke H. Dekofsky, Mary Lyons
courses.description: This course is an introduction to nutrition science, integrating the relationship between human nutrition and health or disease states including the integration of anatomy, physiology, chemistry, biochemistry, medical science, and biotechnology to understand the full impact on the human body. Current nutrition guidance models and approaches to medical nutrition are analyzed with an emphasis on evidence-based findings. Food selection, the nutritional components of and its physiological function, metabolism, and risks associated with excesses or deficiencies throughout the lifecycle are also discussed. The impact of technological advances in nutrition is explored, along with a discussion of a lack of equity in access to food and nutrition information, fundamental principles of nutrition utilizing the scientific method and dietary approaches to health and wellness. Prerequisite: TI Nutrition Science 110<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Nutrition Science AD Eligibility for English 1A/English C1000 or equivalent NT Formerly Nutrition and Foods 11

courses.title: Descriptive Intro-Physics
courses.instructor: Mr. Christopher M. Scott
courses.description: This course utilizes lectures and demonstrations to present various principles of physics in a non-mathematical manner. Newton's Laws of motion and gravitation, momentum, work, energy, and heat will be presented. Other topics will be selected from properties of matter, fluids, sound, light, electricity and magnetism, atomic and modern physics, special and general relativity. *Note: Students will not receive UC credit for Physics 11 if taken after Physics 1A or Physics 2A. Prerequisite: TI Physics 11<197>3 Units DS Descriptive Introduction to Physics AD Eligibility for ENGL C1000 or qualification by appropriate AD assessment NT Credit will not be granted to student who has completed a NT college physics course.

courses.title: Autism Adhd Phys/Hlth Imprmnts
courses.instructor: Ms. Kumi Toyoda-Smart
courses.description: This course is designed for students interested in working as a paraprofessional with children who have been diagnosed with autism, Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), children with physical disabilities, and health impairments. Students will be introduced to the social, emotional, cognitive, and academic needs of these children. The causes, signs, symptoms, treatments, and implications of these disorders will be presented. The characteristics of effective teaching methods and the implementation of accommodation strategies in the home and school setting will be explored. The laws governing the rights of children with special needs and their families will be discussed. Students may be required to observe local programs that serve children with special needs. Prerequisite: TI Child Development 165<197>3 Units DS Autism, ADHD, Physical and Health Impairments AD Eligibility for English 1A

courses.instructor: Mr. Thomas A. Hazell
courses.description: This course is designed to provide an intermediate level of instruction and training in fitness assessment and exercise prescription. The course focuses on the physiological adaptations of exercise training/prescriptions, nutrition, ergogenic aids, environmental factors, and assessment concepts to support the students' fitness and exercise level and abilities. Prerequisite: TI Physical Education 10B<197>1 Unit DS Intermediate Body Conditioning and Physical Fitness PR Physical Education 10A with a minimum grade of C or equivalent

courses.title: Business Mathematics
courses.instructor: Karen Green
courses.description: This course is designed to prepare students for mathematical concepts involving quantitative reasoning and analysis in management, finance, accounting, real estate, and other areas of business. The course content involves a thorough study of all aspects of business mathematics including computational skills, percentages, bank reconciliation, use of business formulas and equations, payroll, discounts, and markup/markdowns, simple and compound interest, present values analysis, annuities and sinking funds, credit, depreciation and inventory, payroll taxes, promissory notes, insurance, financial reports, and business statistics. Prerequisite: TI Business 115<197>3 Units DS Business Mathematics PR Mathematics 40 or qualification by appropriate assessment AD English 1 or eligibility for English 1A or qualification by AD appropriate assessment NT Formerly Business 15

courses.title: Liberal Arts Mathematics
courses.instructor: Junko E. Forbes, Ms. Megan Granich, Dr. Hang M. Nguyen, Dr. Matthew Mata
courses.description: This course surveys general ideas and concepts of mathematics, including mathematics of finance, set theory, probability, statistics, voting systems, logic and geometry. Note: This course meets the CSU general education requirement for mathematics and quantitative reasoning. Prerequisite: TI Mathematics 120<197>3 Units DS Nature of Mathematics PR Proficient in intermediate algebra skills NT You must have satisfied the course prerequisite at El Camino NT College or another accredited college. A transcript or NT grade slip is required for verification of a grade from NT another college.

courses.title: Trigonometry
courses.instructor: Dr. Hamza A. Hamza, Michael Bateman, Ms. Lijun P. Wang, Miguel Ornelas, Jr., Elizabeth R. Schwartz
courses.description: This course includes a study of trigonometric functions, their inverses, identities, equations, complex numbers, graphs, and applications. Note: One year of high school geometry is equivalent to Mathematics 60. Prerequisite: TI Mathematics 170<197>3 Units DS Trigonometry PR Mathematics 60 and Mathematics 80 with a minimum grade of C PR or qualification appropriate assessment NT You must have satisfied the course prerequisite at El Camino NT College or another accredited college. A transcript or NT grade slip is required for verification of a grade from NT another college.

courses.title: Precalculus
courses.instructor: Ronald Martinez, Dr. Eduardo Morales, Kaysa Moreno, Kristine Numrich, Dr. Jacquelyn M. Sims
courses.description: This course is a preparation for calculus. Topics of study include polynomial, rational, exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric functions as well as their inverses. Matrices and analytic geometry are introduced, as well as sequences and series. The application of these topics is stressed to enhance conceptual understanding of the material. *Note: The maximum UC credit allowed for students completing Mathematics 180 and Mathematics 130 is one course. Prerequisite: TI Mathematics 180<197>5 Units DS Precalculus PR Mathematics 170 with a minimum grade of C NT You must have satisfied the course prerequisite at El Camino NT College or another accredited college. A transcript or NT grade slip is required for verification of a grade from NT another college.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: TI Mathematics 180<197>5 Units DS Precalculus PR Mathematics 170 with a minimum grade of C NT You must have satisfied the course prerequisite at El Camino NT College or another accredited college.

courses.title: Elementary Geometry
courses.instructor: Gayathri Manikandan
courses.description: This is an introductory course in geometry. Topics include: properties of geometric figures including circles, triangles and other polygons, congruence and similarity, and elementary logical reasoning. Formal proof is introduced and used throughout the course. Note: This course is equivalent to one year of high school geometry. Prerequisite: TI Mathematics 60<197>4 Units DS Elementary Geometry PR Mathematics 40 with a minimum grade of C or credit in PR Mathematics 37 or qualification by appropriate assessment NT This course is equivalent to one year of high school geometry.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: TI Mathematics 60<197>4 Units DS Elementary Geometry PR Mathematics 40 with a minimum grade of C or credit in PR Mathematics 37 or qualification by appropriate assessment NT This course is equivalent to one year of high school geometry.

courses.title: Multi-Variable Calculus
courses.instructor: Mr. Gregory P. Fry, Mr. Robert Eleuteri, Dr. Ashod Minasian, Dr. Jose M. Villalobos, Mr. Paul M. Yun, Dr. Jasmine Ng
courses.description: This course is a study of calculus in several variables including partial differentiation, tangent planes to surfaces, directional derivatives, and optimization problems. Topics include integral calculus in several variables including line, surface, and volume integrals, and the theorems of Green, Gauss (Divergence) and Stokes as generalizations of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus; and topics in vector algebra and solid analytic geometry. Prerequisite: TI Mathematics 220<197>5 Units DS Multi-Variable Calculus PR Mathematics 191 with a minimum grade of C NT You must have satisfied the course prerequisite at El Camino NT College or another accredited college. A transcript or grade NT slip is required for verification or a grade from another NT college.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: TI Mathematics 220<197>5 Units DS Multi-Variable Calculus PR Mathematics 191 with a minimum grade of C NT You must have satisfied the course prerequisite at El Camino NT College or another accredited college.

courses.title: Sgl Var Calc/Anlyt Geometry I
courses.instructor: Dr. Matthew Mata, Dr. Jasmine Ng, Dr. Ashod Minasian, Mr. Guoqiang Song, Mr. Robert Eleuteri, Ms. Lijun P. Wang, Dr. Hamza A. Hamza, Dr. Malinni Roeun, Diaa S. Eldanaf, Gayathri Manikandan, Mr. Gregory P. Fry, Dr. Oscar Villareal
courses.description: Topics in this course include limits, continuity, derivatives and antiderivatives of algebraic and transcendental functions; definite integrals of algebraic and transcendental functions with and without the fundamental theorem of calculus; linear approximations; relating features of a function's graph to its derivatives; and application problems using derivatives as well as implicit differentiation. Problem solving using computer software is also addressed. *Note: The maximum UC credit allowed for students completing Mathematics 160 and 161 and Mathematics 190 and 191 is one course. Prerequisite: TI Mathematics 190<197>5 Units DS Single Variable Calculus and Analytic Geometry I PR Mathematics 180 with a minimum grade of C or qualification by PR testing (El Camino College Mathematics Placement Test) and PR assessment NT You must have satisfied the course prerequisite at El Camino NT College or another accredited college. A transcript or grade NT slip is required for verification of a grade from another NT college.

courses.title: Calc for Bus and Social Sci
courses.instructor: Gayathri Manikandan, Ms. Megan Granich, Dr. Jasmine Ng, Dr. Le Gui, Mr. Eduardo J. Barajas
courses.description: This course consists of an introduction to differential and integral calculus with business and social science applications to include polynomial, rational, exponential and logarithmic functions and their graphs; multi-variable calculus to include partial differentiation, optimization, and introduction to differential equations. Prerequisite: TI Mathematics 165<197>5 Units DS Calculus for Business and Social Sciences PR Mathematics 130 or Mathematics 180 with a minimum grade of C PR in prerequisite or qualification by testing (El Camino PR College Mathematics Placement Test) and assessment

courses.title: Beauty of Comptr Sci Prncpls
courses.instructor: Julie Wood
courses.description: This course introduces students to the central ideas of computer science, inviting students to develop the computational thinking vital for success in computer programming and across multiple disciplines. The course is unique in its focus on fostering students to be creative and encouraging students to apply innovative processes when developing computational artifacts such as avisualization, graphic or movie. Students will design and program inventive solutions to computational problems using an iterative process similar to what computer scientists, engineers, artists, and writers use to bring ideas to life. Prerequisite: TI Computer Science 7<197>4 Units DS The Beauty of Computer Science Principles PR credit in Mathematics 37 or Mathematics 40 with a minimum PR grade of C in prerequisite or qualification by testing (El PR Camino College Mathematics Placement Test) and assessment

courses.instructor: Ms. Sabine V. Huemer, Dr. Yun Chu, Mr. Micheal J. Huff, Mr. Kevin Dooley
courses.description: This course is centered on the philosophy of science in general and the scientific method in particular. Students develop individual research studies with these elements: literature review, hypothesis, design and method, data collection and analysis, and discussion, oral presentation, and manuscript preparation (APA Publication Style). Note: Psychology 109B is the same course as SOCI 109B. Prerequisite: TI Psychology 109B<197>4 Units DS Research Methods in the Behavioral Sciences PR Psychology 109A or Sociology 109A; Mathematics 150 or PR Mathematics 150H with a minimum grade of C NT formerly Psychology 9B

courses.title: Statistics for Business Decision Making
courses.instructor: Ms. Melissa Forrester
courses.description: This course takes a business focused approach to statistical analysis. Students will collect, clean, process, present, and interpret numerical data for the purpose of making effective business decisions. Key topics include: hypothesis testing, measures of central value and spread, confidence intervals, development of projections, probability distributions, sampling methods, hypothesis testing, Analysis of Variance (ANOVA), regression and correlation. Students will apply these methodologies and use inferential thinking to make decisions in areas such as operations, marketing, sales, economics, and human resources. Prerequisite: TI Business 116<197>3 Units DS Statistics for Business Decision Making PR Intermediate algebra, has met the standards of the Common Core PR State Standards for Mathematics (CCSSM) from high school, or PR equivalent

courses.title: Introduction to Statistics
courses.instructor: Dr. Pavan Nagpal, Diaa S. Eldanaf, Dr. Eduardo Morales, Catherine Schult Roman, Kaysa Moreno, Dr. Le Gui, Mr. Paul M. Yun, Benjamin Mitchell, Ronald Martinez, Junko E. Forbes, Ms. Megan Granich, Ms. Alice M. Martinez, Susana Acuna-Acosta, Mr. Eduardo J. Barajas, Michael Bateman, Kristine Numrich
courses.description: This course is an introduction to statistical thinking and processes, including methods and concepts for discovery and decision-making using data. Topics include descriptive statistics; probability and sampling distributions; statistical inference; correlation and linear regression; analysis of variance, chi-squared, and t-tests; and application of technology for statistical analysis including the interpretation of the relevance of the statistical findings. Students apply methods and processes to applications using data from a broad range of disciplines. Prerequisite: TI Statistics C1000<197>4 Units DS Introduction to Statistics PR Placement as determined by the college's multiple measures assessment process or completion of a course taught at or above the level of intermediate algebra. NT You must have satisfied the course prerequisite at El Camino NT College or another accredited college. A transcript or NT grade slip is required for verification of a grade from NT another college. NT formerly Mathematics 150

courses.title: Creative Wrtng: Screenwriting
courses.instructor: Ms. Stephanie M. Schwartz
courses.description: In this course students learn the skills necessary to create original feature film screenplays. Themes, character, plot, and dialogue are discussed and developed by means of writing exercises. Script format and story structure are analyzed by reading feature film scripts and discussing produced film stories. Prerequisite: TI English 78<197>3 Units DS Creative Writing: Screenwriting PR eligibility for English 1A NT formerly English 38ab

courses.title: Children's Literature
courses.instructor: Elayne R. Kelley
courses.description: This course introduces representative works of children's literature and develops students' close reading and analytical writing skills while promoting an appreciation for diverse texts and for the aesthetic and literary qualities of literature written for children. It includes critical approaches and the examination of shared themes. Students read both classic and contemporary works and reflect on the significance of a genre written specifically for children. Prerequisite: TI English 27<197>3 Units DS Children's Literature AD Eligibility for English C1000 or qualification by appropriate AD assessment

courses.title: Creatv Wrtng:intro Fict
courses.instructor: Mora Mattern, Ms. Jennifer Gallagher
courses.description: This course is an introductory level creative writing course focusing on writing the short story. In addition to outlining and composing well-crafted stories, students will study the techniques of plot, characterization, point of view, conflict, and setting. Students will also learn about peer editing and the workshop model for revising stories. Note: Letter grade or pass/no pass option. Prerequisite: TI English 25A<197>3 Units DS Creative Writing: Introduction to the Craft of Fiction AD English 1 or eligibility for English 1A or qualification by AD appropriate assessment

courses.title: World Lit: 3500 BCE - 1650 CE
courses.instructor: Mr. Brent M. Isaacs
courses.description: This course examines masterpieces of world literature from the ancient world through the mid-17th century as both reflections and progenitors of their cultures and, eventually, nations of origin. Students explore the universal concerns of humankind as exemplified through the themes, forms, and trends of the literary works. Prerequisite: TI English 35<197>3 Units DS World Literature: 3500 BCE to 1650 CE PR Eligibility for English C1000 or qualification by appropriate PR assessment

courses.title: Intro to Creative Nonfiction
courses.instructor: Mr. Michael T. McDermit
courses.description: This course is an introduction to creative nonfiction, which includes memoir writing, literary nonfiction, and the personal essay. Students will study the techniques of creative nonfiction through the examination of diverse authors and will work towards transforming real-life events and experiences into finished narratives using the same techniques as fiction, such as character development, dialogue, setting, and conflict. Students will also practice the creative process, including writing exercises, peer workshop, and revision. Prerequisite: TI English 23A<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Creative Nonfiction AD eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Inter Read/Vocab Bldg
courses.instructor: Ms. Rebecca A. Loya
courses.description: This is an intermediate level course designed to teach appropriate reading skills, such as the ability to make inferences, draw conclusions, understand the meaning of vocabulary words in context through word analysis and contextual clues, and determine the main idea from a passage. Students read from a variety of sources, including newspapers, essays, short stories, and novels. Students also increase their academic vocabulary through vocabulary building activities. Prerequisite: TI English as a Second Language 52B<197>5 Units DS Intermediate Reading and Vocabulary Building PR ESL 52A with a minimum grade of C or Noncredit English as a PR Second Language 52A with a Pass or qualification by assessment

courses.title: Adv Essay Writing and Grammar
courses.instructor: Dr. Saeid Atoofi, Dr. Jenny L. Simon
courses.description: This course strengthens college-level critical reading, writing, and research skills in preparation for English 1A for English learners. It develops students' abilities to analyze and respond to texts from culturally diverse traditions. Students read articles, essays, poetry, and works of fiction or non-fiction which are of literary and cultural value. In response to these texts, students write approximately four academic essays (including at least one in-class essay) of at least 750 words each in which they support a thesis using textual evidence. Students review advanced grammar and sentence skills. In addition, students develop critical research strategies to write a thesis-driven, multi-source MLA-style research paper. Prerequisite: TI English as a Second Language 53C<197>5 Units DS Advanced Essay Writing and Grammar PR English as a Second Language 53B with a minimum grade of C PR or Noncredit English as a Second Language 53B or qualification PR by assessment AD English as a Second Language 52B or Noncredit English as a AD Second Language 52B

courses.title: Academic Reading & Writing-Honors
courses.instructor: Ms. Stephanie Burnham, Ms. Nida Husain, Ms. Robin Arehart
courses.description: In this course, students receive instruction in academic reading and writing, including writing processes, effective use of language, analytical thinking, and the foundations of academic research. This is an honors course. Emphasis will be placed on the ability to write an essay in which each paragraph relates to a controlling idea, has an introduction and conclusion, and contains primary and secondary support. College-level reading material will be assigned to provide the stimulus for class discussion and writing assignments, including a required research paper. This course is enriched through extensive, rigorous reading, writing, and research assignments. Note: Students may take either English 1A or English 1AH. Duplicate credit will not be awarded for English 1A and English 1AH. Prerequisite: TI English C1000H<197>4 Units DS Academic Reading and Writing - Honors PR Placement as determined by the college's multiple measures PR assessment process. NT Students may take either English 1A or English 1AH. NT Duplicate credit will not be awarded for English 1A and NT English 1AH. NT formerly English 1AH

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: TI English C1000H<197>4 Units DS Academic Reading and Writing - Honors PR Placement as determined by the college's multiple measures PR assessment process.

courses.instructor: Hatien D. Nguyen, Ms. Amanda M. Colbeck, Ms. Lauren K. Takahashi
courses.description: This course provides students with the information, skills, and resources necessary for successful educational experiences. Students will become aware of their responsibilities as students in a diverse college setting, develop an understanding of their individual learning styles, create realistic and obtainable educational and career goals, develop skills in managing time to achieve goals, and learn how to create a support network using college resources and services. Prerequisite: TI Human Development 101<197>1 Unit DS Orientation to College and Educational Planning AD English 84 or English as a Second Language 52B; English A AD or English as a Second Language 53C NT formerly Human Development 8

courses.instructor: Dr. Amina Y. Humphrey, Ms. Jessica M. Rodriguez, Mr. Xocoyotzin Herrera, Dr. Jub U. Sankofa, Mr. Jose Arrieta, Mr. Raul M. Herrera, Dr. Takahito Tanaka, Mr. Michael Fraga, Mr. Nathan A. Lopez, Naomi Ramirez, Mr. Ricardo D. Munguia, Mr. Ismael De La O, Justice Isaacs
courses.description: This course provides a multidisciplinary introduction and analysis of ethnic groups in the United States. Prerequisite: TI Ethnic Studies 1<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Ethnic Studies AD Eligibility for English 1A/English C1000 or equivalent

courses.title: Sport and Society
courses.instructor: Mr. Thomas A. Hazell, Matthew B. Kirk, Jessica Rapoza, Adam Nyssen
courses.description: This course examines the role of sport in modern society. Students will reflect on the ways in which sport influences and shapes global attitudes among nations. It also investigates the historical, social, economic, and political impact of sport on society. Prerequisite: TI Physical Education 212<197>3 Units DS Sport and Society AD English 1 or eligibility for English 1A or qualification by appropriate assessment

courses.title: Consitutional Law/Crmnal Just
courses.instructor: Oscar E. Serrano
courses.description: In this course, students examine the history and development of the laws and customs of arrest, search and seizure, and the Exclusionary Rule. Emphasis is given to the application of these procedures as they relate to law enforcement officers and others in the apprehension of criminals and the procuring of evidence. Prerequisite: TI Administration of Justice 170<197>3 Units DS Constitutional Law for Criminal Justice AD Eligibility for English 1A/English C1000 or equivalent

courses.title: Intro to Biological Anthropology
courses.instructor: Mr. Billy W. Wong, Mr. Lawrence J. Ramirez, Dr. Rodolfo A. Otero, Mr. Vincent S. Desetto, Marianne Waters, Ms. Dianna D. Stifter, Alberto Vigil, Ms. Kirsten Olson
courses.description: This course explores and emphasizes the evolution and biological diversity of the human species and our closest living relatives, the non-human primates. Topics include genetics, mechanisms of evolutionary change, primate behavior and ecology, human biological variation, and human evolutionary history through examination of the fossil record. Prerequisite: TI Anthropology 1<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Biological Anthropology AD English 1 or eligibility for English 1A or qualification by AD appropriate assessment

courses.title: Intro Cultural Anthropology
courses.instructor: Angela C. Mannen, Mr. Lawrence J. Ramirez, Alberto Vigil
courses.description: This course will provide students with an introduction to culture theory as well as to the cross-cultural understanding of human behavior. Emphasis will be placed on subsistence strategies; marriage and kinship institutions; levels of sociopolitical organization; religious systems; and the effects of cultural change, mainly as the result of European expansion, industrialization, and globalization. Prerequisite: TI Anthropology 2<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Cultural Anthropology AD English 1 or eligibility for English 1A or qualification by AD appropriate assessment

courses.title: Bldg Constrct-Fire Protct
courses.instructor: Tracy Rickman, Mr. Terry J. Tuzzolino, Jr.
courses.description: This course is the study of the components of building construction that relate to fire safety. The elements of construction and design of structures are shown to be key factors when inspecting buildings, pre-planning fire operations, and operating at fires. The development and evolution of building and fire codes will be studied in relationship to past fires in residential, commercial, and industrial occupancies. Prerequisite: TI Fire and Emergency Technology 6<197>3 Units DS Building Construction for Fire Protection AD Fire and Emergency Technology 1 and Eligibility for English AD 1A/English C1000

courses.title: Hist West: Proren to 19 Cent Hist West:proren-19 Cent
courses.instructor: Ms. Karen Beckstead, Elizabeth Russell
courses.description: This course surveys the history and development of Western painting, sculpture, and architecture in relation to changing social, political, religious, and philosophical conditions from the 14th through the 19th centuries. The focus is on the Proto-Renaissance, Renaissance, Mannerism, Baroque, Rococo, and Neoclassical periods in Italy, Spain, Flanders, The Netherlands, France, England, Germany and the New World. Prerequisite: TI Art History 102B<197>3 Units DS History of Western Art - Proto-Renaissance to 19th Century AD English 1 or eligibility for English 1A or qualification by AD appropriate assessment NT formerly Art 102B

courses.title: Introduction to Archaeology
courses.instructor: Ms. Kirsten Olson
courses.description: This course is a survey of the field of archaeology in the United States and abroad. It traces the history of archaeology and reviews the concepts, topics of concern, and research methodologies commonly encountered within the field of archaeology. Students will be introduced to basic techniques of soils analysis and archaeological materials analysis. Prerequisite: TI Anthropology 3<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Archaeology AD English 1 or eligibility for English 1A or qualification by AD appropriate assessment

courses.title: Research Skills in the Digital Age
courses.instructor: Ms. Camila M. Jenkin
courses.description: This course introduces students to college-level research skills and academic libraries. Students will develop information literacy and critical thinking competencies through the practice of finding and evaluating information, recognizing credible sources, and giving credit for ideas through citations. Students will expand skills using digital tools to successfully complete a research assignment in future classes. This class will examine power structures in information and help students navigate information overload. Prerequisite: TI Library Information Science 100<197>1 Unit DS Research Skills in the Digital Age AD Eligibility for English 1A/English C1000 or equivalent NT Formerly Library Information Science 1

courses.title: Career and Life Planning
courses.instructor: Nikki L. Barber, Ms. Chanae' O. Wami
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to career and life planning, including an exploration of core values, skills, personality traits, life stages and experiences. Students will develop a career and life plan by applying career planning techniques and decision-making strategies. Emphasis will be placed on awareness of psychological, sociological, and physiological factors related to career and life. Prerequisite: TI Human Development 105<197>1 Unit DS Career and Life Planning AD Eligibility for English 1A/English C1000 or equivalent NT formerly Human Development 5

courses.title: Critical Thinking and Writing
courses.instructor: Ms. Stephanie Burnham, Ms. Dana Crotwell, Mr. Shane Ochoa, Mr. Christopher J. Page, Kevin M. Degnan, Ms. Lana Phillips, Jane Sandor, Ms. Anna C. Mavromati Duncan, Ms. Jocelyn Shaw, Mr. Jawad Ali, Ms. Kamisha Sullivan, Ms. Angie M. Kirk, Mr. Ryan D. Sandowicz, Dr. Stephanie Merz, Ms. Victoria L. Cliett, Ms. Elice C. Hennessee, Mr. Michael T. McDermit, Ms. Nida Husain, Susan Herdzina, Ms. Aura Imbarus, Mora Mattern, Ms. Cathleen Daniel, Danielle D. Panto, Mr. Peter M. Marcoux, Ms. Claire Phillips, Mr. Michael A. Reyes, Elayne R. Kelley, Mr. Matthew G. Cheung, Mr. Jeffrey McMahon, Dr. Chelsea Henson, Ms. Jennifer Annick, Kim D. Runkle, Jason Bostick, Mr. Ryan L. Ritchie
courses.description: In this course, students receive instruction in critical thinking for purposes of constructing, evaluating, and composing arguments in a variety of rhetorical forms, using primarily non-fiction texts, refining writing skills and research strategies developed in ENGL C1000 College Reading and Writing (or C-ID ENGL 100) or similar first-year college writing course. Students will learn to evaluate and interpret data, to recognize assumptions, to distinguish facts from opinions, to identify and avoid logical fallacies, to identify and employ deductive and inductive reasoning, and to effectively assert and support argumentative claims Prerequisite: TI English C1001<197>4 Units DS Critical Thinking and Writing PR College-level composition (ENGL C1000/ ENGL C1000H/ ENGL PR C1000E/C-ID ENGL 100) or equivalent NT This course satisfies the critical thinking/English NT composition requirement of IGETC and the critical thinking NT requirement of the CSU transfer pattern. NT formerly English 1C

courses.title: Career Dev Across the Lifespan
courses.instructor: Ms. Amanda M. Colbeck, Mr. Emmanuel Aguiar, Hatien D. Nguyen
courses.description: This course offers a comprehensive approach to career development across the lifespan. Theories of career and life development provide a framework for understanding vocational choice, work satisfaction, and career transition. Topics include psychological and sociological factors influencing education, career and personal decision-making, resources and assessment tools related to college majors and careers, and the knowledge, skills and personal qualities necessary for success in a diverse workplace. The course also reviews changing global environments, labor market trends, career research, and job search strategies. Prerequisite: TI Human Development 115<197>3 Units DS Career Development Across the Lifespan AD Eligibility for English 1A/English C1000 or equivalent

courses.title: Intro to Women's Studies
courses.instructor: Ms. Allison Baker, Ms. Stacey Allen, Dr. Melissa Fujiwara
courses.description: This interdisciplinary course introduces students to the field of women's studies. The history of feminism, feminist thought, and women's studies as an academic discipline will be examined and assessed. The social construction of sex, gender, and sexuality, the representation of women in American society, and the role of women in American institutions will be analyzed from theoretical perspectives of various academic disciplines. Feminist perspectives will be utilized to critically analyze race, ethnicity, social class, sexuality, and age as they correlate with sex and gender in American society. Prerequisite: TI Women's Studies 1<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Women's Studies AD Eligibility for English 1A/English C1000 or equivalent

courses.title: Academic Reading and Writing
courses.instructor: Dr. Stephanie Merz, Mr. Shane Ochoa, Elayne R. Kelley, Ms. Rachel Williams, Dr. Sarah M. Gray, Mr. Peter M. Marcoux, Ms. Lana Phillips, Mr. Ryan L. Ritchie, Mr. Jeffrey McMahon, Mr. Salvatore D. Russo, Miles Magnesi, Mr. Stephen Peluso, Clinton Margrave, Ms. Sarah E. Leinen, Ms. Adrienne S. Sharp, Ms. Jennifer Annick, Susan Herdzina, Dr. Chelsea Henson, Dr. Christina Nagao, Ms. Claire Phillips, Ms. Jennifer Gallagher, Ms. Robin Arehart, Danielle D. Panto, Jane Sandor, Mora Mattern, Dr. Allison A. Carr, Ms. Kamisha Sullivan, Joanne Hall, Tiffany D. Huynh, Mr. Jeffrey Douglas, Mr. Jawad Ali, Mr. John T. Millea, Ms. Susan M. Corbin, Ms. Cathleen Daniel, Ms. Angie M. Kirk, Jennifer J. Holt-Molina
courses.description: In this course, students receive instruction in academic reading and writing, including writing processes, effective use of language, analytical thinking, and the foundations of academic research. Emphasis will be placed on the ability to write an essay in which each paragraph relates to a controlling idea, has an introduction and conclusion, and contains primary and secondary support. College-level reading material will be assigned to provide the stimulus for class discussion and writing assignments, including a required research paper. Prerequisite: TI English C1000<197>4 Units DS Academic Reading and Wriiting PR Placement as determined by the college's multiple measures PR assessment process. NT formerly English 1A

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: TI English C1000<197>4 Units DS Academic Reading and Wriiting PR Placement as determined by the college's multiple measures PR assessment process.

courses.title: Elementary Japanese I
courses.instructor: Ms. Aiko Sato, Yuka Kitazono, Mari Lopez, Mr. Kanzo Takemori
courses.description: This course, taught within the context of Japanese culture, introduces students to the development of skills for language acquisition in speaking, listening, reading, and writing of modern Japanese. Students also are introduced to the cultural background of the language. Note: This course is comparable to two years of high school Japanese. Prerequisite: TI Japanese 1<197>5 Units DS Elementary Japanese I AD English 1 or eligibility for English A or qualification by AD appropriate assessment NT Approved by State Department of Education as an ethnic NT studies course. This course is comparable to two years of NT high school Japanese.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: TI Japanese 1<197>5 Units DS Elementary Japanese I AD English 1 or eligibility for English A or qualification by AD appropriate assessment NT Approved by State Department of Education as an ethnic NT studies course.

courses.title: Elementary French I
courses.instructor: Ms. Zeina Chakhchir
courses.description: This course introduces students to speaking, listening, reading and writing linguistically appropriate French. Students are also introduced to the cultural background of the language, including the relationships among cultural practices and perspectives and general aspects of everyday life. Technological support may include videos, DVDs, audio CDs, and Internet access to publishers' websites for tutoring and other support. Note: This course is comparable to two years of high school French. Prerequisite: TI French 1<197>5 Units DS Elementary French I AD English 1 or eligibility for English A or qualification by AD appropriate assessment NT This course is comparable to two years of high school French.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: TI French 1<197>5 Units DS Elementary French I AD English 1 or eligibility for English A or qualification by AD appropriate assessment NT This course is comparable to two years of high school French.

courses.title: Persnl/Communty Health Issues
courses.instructor: Robert C. Uphoff, Mr. Gifford Lindheim, Karla A. Calderon, Kirsten M. Green, Andrew A. Alvillar, Mr. Thomas A. Hazell
courses.description: This course is designed to provide a critical analysis of factors, which affect personal and community health. Primary emphasis is placed upon self-empowerment and disease prevention in a culturally diverse community. General topics include infectious and non-infectious diseases; physical fitness, weight management, and nutrition; human reproduction and sexuality; stress management and mental health; drug use and abuse; and environmental health. Note: This course satisfies Section A, Area 5 of the associate degrees' requirements. Note: The maximum UC credit allowed for students completing Contemporary Health 1 and Contemporary Health 5 is one course. Prerequisite: TI Contemporary Health 1<197>3 Units DS Personal and Community Health Issues AD English 84 and English B

courses.title: Underwater Robotics
courses.instructor: Joseph A. Weichman
courses.description: This introductory course provides students with a general overview of the underwater robotics industry and its science, design and fabrication. Students will explore the types of underwater vehicles, how they are designed, how robots function in a water environment, and the types of structures and materials used for underwater robotics. Students will also comprehend the pressure hulls and canisters necessary in underwater robots, as well as comprehend buoyancy, stability and ballast, and how it impacts maneuverability in an ocean environment. The curriculum will guide students in understanding the power systems used in underwater robotics, as well as selection criteria involving navigation, control, hydraulics, payloads and operations of underwater robots. Prerequisite: TI Robotics 120<197>4 Units DS Underwater Robotics AD Eligibility for English 1A/English C1000

courses.title: Weather and Climate
courses.instructor: Dr. Julienne Gard
courses.description: This course is a survey of the causes and regional distribution of the Earth's weather and climate. The content includes the atmosphere, cause of seasons, air temperature, air pressure and wind, clouds and precipitation, and storms. Use of weather instruments and weather data interpretation are also covered. Prerequisite: TI Geography 9<197>3 Units DS Weather and Climate AD English 82

courses.instructor: Jason W. Scott, Dr. Julienne Gard, Jose Diminutto, Ms. Sara E. Avrit, Kane H. Diaz

courses.title: Language and Culture
courses.instructor: Marianne Waters
courses.description: This course introduces students to the anthropological study of language. Using a cross-cultural perspective, students will examine the relationship between culture and the ways in which humans communicate. Topics include language acquisition, structure, diversity, and change. The social and non-verbal aspects of language will also be explored. Prerequisite: TI Anthropology 4<197>3 Units DS Language and Culture AD eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Criminal Law I
courses.instructor: Mr. Mark C. Fields, Mr. Andrew N. Gulcher, Mr. Jose F. Rosales
courses.description: In this course, students examine the historical development and philosophy of law and its application to the criminal justice system. Topics covered include legal research, case law, classifications of crime, crimes against persons and property, and crimes against the state. Prerequisite: TI Administration of Justice 103<197>3 Units DS Concepts of Criminal Law I AD Eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Criminal Investigation
courses.instructor: Mr. Mark C. Fields, Dina Mauger
courses.description: This course is a study of the fundamentals of criminal investigation. Topics covered include the techniques of crime scene search and recording, collection and preservation of physical evidence, modus operandi processes, sources of information, interview and interrogation, and follow-up investigation. Prerequisite: TI Administration of Justice 111<197>3 Units DS Criminal Investigation AD Administration of Justice 109 AND Eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Social Problems
courses.instructor: Ms. Michelle Bell-Blossom, Ms. Corina Diaz, Ms. Mediha M. Din
courses.description: In this course students examine social problems facing societies today. Topics include problems associated with deviance, crime, inequality, social institutions, and modernization. Social problems will be analyzed from various sociological perspectives to better understand their possible causes and consequences. Strategies for addressing social problems will be discussed and assessed. Prerequisite: TI Sociology 104<197>3 Units DS Social Problems AD Sociology 101; eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: History of African Art
courses.instructor: Mr. Walter J. Meyer
courses.description: This course is an introductory survey of African art in relation to its historical and cultural contexts. It focuses on specific groups and regions with an emphasis on sub- Sahara West and Central Africa, stressing the different roles of the visual arts within each culture. Prerequisite: TI Art History 209<197>3 Units DS History of African Art AD eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Art 209

courses.title: Criminal Procedures
courses.instructor: Seth D. Fogel, Ms. Andrea A. Roberson, Brian K. Juckett
courses.description: This course is an in-depth study of the procedures by which a criminal defendant is tried. As enumerated by the Constitution's due process clauses of the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments, this course examines how this process functions from the point at which a crime is committed to arrest, trial and appeal. Prerequisite: TI Administration of Justice 130<197>3 Units DS Criminal Procedures AD Eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Fire Prevention Technolgy
courses.instructor: Matthew P. Hallock, Tracy Rickman
courses.description: This course provides fundamental information regarding the history and philosophy of fire prevention; organization and operation of a fire prevention bureau; use of fire codes; identification and correction of fire hazards; and the relationship of fire prevention with fire safety education and detection and suppression systems. Prerequisite: TI Fire and Emergency Technology 2<197>3 Units DS Fire Prevention Technology AD Fire and Emergency Technology 1 and eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: History of Rock Music
courses.instructor: Dr. David M. Moyer, Mr. Ryota J. Minei, Dr. Margot Martin
courses.description: This course focuses on the techniques, styles, composers/performers, and evolution of rock and roll from the 1950s to the present. Emphasis is placed on the roots and early development of rock music, its stylistic trends, influential artists, and the impact of technology on rock music throughout the years. Prerequisite: TI Music 116<197>3 Units DS History of Rock Music AD Eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Music 16

courses.title: Small Group Communication
courses.instructor: Ms. Daryle D. Nagano, Julia Anderson, Dr. Julia Matthews, Valerie Ortega, Ms. Helene Wagner, Mr. Kelly Janke
courses.description: In this course students plan and participate in a variety of group projects, such as panel and symposium discussions. Topics include decision making, role identification, interpersonal needs within a group, team participation, appropriate interdependency on group members, effective leadership skills, cohesiveness, conflict resolution and professional presentation styles. Prerequisite: TI Communication Studies 140<197>3 Units DS Small Group Communication AD eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Communication Studies 3

courses.title: Marketing
courses.instructor: Karen Green, Mr. Joshua D. Escalante Troesh
courses.description: This course is an introduction to marketing principles and explores marketing concepts and strategies. Topics include market analysis, planning, product development, marketing mix, and media decisions. Electronic marketing and the environmental factors of marketing including social, ethical, and legal considerations are also studied. Note: Letter grade or pass/no pass option. Prerequisite: TI Business 110<197>3 Units DS Marketing AD Eligibility for English C1000 NT Formerly Business 14

courses.title: Intro Terrorism/Counterrorism
courses.instructor: Brian K. Juckett
courses.description: This course will provide students with an overview of domestic and transnational terrorism. Varying definitions, perspectives, and underlying causes will be explored along with terrorist weapons of choice. Terrorist and counterterrorist organizations will be examined, as well as incidents involving terrorist efforts, successes, and failures. Prerequisite: TI Administration of Justice 26<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Terrorism and Counterterrorism AD Administration of Justice 11 AND eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Medical Terminology
courses.instructor: Michele M. Green, Randy Wade, Ian Jenkins, Taryn N. Parker, Victoria E. Robertson
courses.description: This course provides study and practical application of a medical vocabulary system according to body systems. Students review the basic construction of medical words, concentrating on word origins, root words, prefixes, and suffixes. Word structure, recognition, analysis, definition, spelling, and pronunciation are presented in the context of medical terms for organs, diseases, symptoms, diagnostic tests, and medical surgical procedures. Prerequisite: TI Medical Terminology 1<197>3 Units DS Medical Terminology AD Eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Intro Police Patrl Procdr
courses.instructor: Alfred D. Martinez, Oscar E. Serrano
courses.description: This course covers the study of the history and development of police patrol. Topics covered include functions of patrol, investigative duties of the field officer, techniques for planning patrol activities, handling complaints and requests for service, field interviews, searches and arrests, civil and domestic disturbances, and the handling of traffic related problems. Prerequisite: TI Administration of Justice 109<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Police Patrol Procedures AD Eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Cultural Psychology
courses.instructor: Ms. Arianna Yates
courses.description: This course will examine psychological principles, theory, and research through exploration of cultural differences and similarities, both within and across cultures. Topics may include the interplay between culture and developmental processes, cognition, emotion, communication, gender, personality development, psychopathology, and social behavior. Prerequisite: TI Psychology 117<197>3 Units DS Cultural Psychology PR Psychology C1000 or Psychology C1000H with a minimum grade of PR C AD Eligibility for English C1000 NT formerly Psychology 17

courses.title: Introduction to Business
courses.instructor: Ms. Melissa Som de Cerff, Mr. Yuen K. Chau, Dr. John Mufich, Mr. Joshua D. Escalante Troesh, Ms. Emily De Ruyter
courses.description: This course is a survey of organization and management factors influencing the establishment, location, and operation of businesses. This course emphasizes the functional area of accounting as well as the legal framework within which business activities are conducted. The course also emphasizes the principles of Total Quality Management (TQM) in a multicultural and globalized environment. Prerequisite: TI Business 101<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Business AD Eligibility for English C1000 NT Formerly Business 25

courses.title: Dance Appreciation
courses.instructor: Mr. Daniel E. Berney, Mr. Jonathan P. Bryant, Ms. Elizabeth H. Adamis
courses.description: This course is a survey of styles, artists, and cultural/historical context for dance as an art form and social expression. Topics of emphasis may be varied from semester to semester to reflect visiting artists, current events and popular culture. In addition to class time, students will be required to attend selected dance events. Prerequisite: TI Dance 101<197>3 Units DS Dance Appreciation AD eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Dance 1

courses.title: Anthro Religion/Magic/Wtchcrft
courses.instructor: Dr. Rodolfo A. Otero
courses.description: This course is a cross-cultural and evolutionary survey of the supernatural. It includes an examination of magic and witchcraft practices and the different functions they play in various world societies including hunting and gathering groups, tribal level and state level societies. Additionally, religious beliefs, symbolism and ritual are explained from an anthropological perspective. Prerequisite: TI Anthropology 11<197>3 Units DS Anthropology of Religion, Magic and Witchcraft AD eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Intro to Corrections
courses.instructor: Mr. Vincent E. Plair
courses.description: This is a basic course covering crime, criminal behavior and social attempts at controlling that behavior. Emphasis in this course is placed on traditional types of punishment, as well as rehabilitation, probation, parole, and diversion. This course covers the basic career skills and knowledge required for employment. Prerequisite: TI Administration of Justice 107<197>3 Units DS Crime and Control - An Introduction to Corrections AD Eligibility for English 1A NT UC transfer credit effective FA 2024

courses.title: Survey of Art: Prehistory-Medieval Era
courses.instructor: Ms. Lucy Alamillo
courses.description: This course introduces students to visual art and architecture from prehistory to the medieval era with a focus on art from Europe, North Africa, and the near East. The course will further consider global interactions involving these regions. Prerequisite: TI Art History C1100<197>3 Units DS Survey of Art from Prehistory to the Medieval Era AD Eligibility for English C1000 or qualification by appropriate AD assessment NT formerly ART 102A/AHIS 102A

courses.title: African Amercn Psychology
courses.instructor: Ms. Jasmine M. Phillips, Ms. Angel Hall
courses.description: This course is an introduction to the psychological issues related to the African American experience in the United States. The relationship between the African American experience and social perception, social cognition, and identity and attitude formation is emphasized. Individual cognitive styles, personality development, and family structures of African Americans will also be examined. Prerequisite: TI Psychology 110<197>3 Units DS African American Psychology AD Eligibility for English C1000 NT formerly Psychology 10

courses.title: Ethnicity Amer Polit Proc
courses.instructor: Mr. Van Chaney
courses.description: In this course students will analyze racial and ethnic group relations in the context of the American political system and process. The relationship of racial and ethnic groups in American society to local, state, and national governments will be examined. Emphasis is placed on problems of assimilation and integration into the American political system. Prerequisite: TI Political Science 5<197>3 Units DS Ethnicity in the American Political Process AD Eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Legal Aspects of Evidence
courses.instructor: Ms. Andrea A. Roberson, Mr. Donald Mason
courses.description: This course covers the legal aspects evidence and the constitutional and procedural considerations affecting arrest, and search and seizure. Topics covered include types of evidence and rules governing their admissibility, judicial decisions interpreting individual rights, and case studies viewed with respect to how they are applied in a criminal process from arrest to trial. Prerequisite: TI Administration of Justice 131<197>3 Units DS Legal Aspects of Evidence AD Eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Lifespan Development
courses.instructor: Mr. Bradfield E. Conn, Mr. Micheal J. Huff, Dr. Amy J. Himsel, Daniel J. Pueblos, Ms. Jasmine M. Phillips
courses.description: This course examines physical, cognitive and psychosocial development throughout the human lifespan from conception to death. Special emphasis is placed on culture, family relationships, and the interplay of genes and environment. Attention is also devoted to the practical application of research findings to ongoing developmental issues. Prerequisite: TI Psychology 116<197>3 Units DS Lifespan Development PR Psychology C1000 or Psychology C1000H with a minimum grade of PR C AD Eligibility for English C1000 NT formerly Psychology 16

courses.title: United States History Since 1865
courses.instructor: Mr. Paul D. Swendson, Mr. Dustin Black, Mr. Muhammad Rafi, Mr. Mark T. Wishon, Dr. Orion Teal
courses.description: This course is a historical survey of the United States from the end of the Civil War to the present. The course also introduces students to historical reasoning skills. Note: Students may take either History C1002 or History C1002H. Students can take History C1002 or C1002H without having taken History C1001 or History C1001H. Duplicate credit will not be awarded. Prerequisite: TI History C1002<197>3 Units DS United States History since 1865 AD Eligibility for English C1000 or equivalent NT formerly HIST 102

courses.title: Intro-Intercultrl Commun
courses.instructor: Larry R. Leach, Ms. Fariba Sadeghi-Tabrizi
courses.description: This course examines the study, practice, and application of general communication theories; cross-cultural communication styles; and cultural, ethnic and individual identity development, including African American, Native American, Asian, Latinx, LGBTQ+, and other non-dominant cultures. Intercultural communication is enhanced through assignments that facilitate competence and minimize prejudice and discrimination in various cross-cultural communication contexts. Prerequisite: TI Communication Studies 260<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Intercultural Communication AD eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Communication Studies 14

courses.title: Fitness and Sports Nutrition
courses.instructor: Mr. Thomas A. Hazell, Michael A. Grissett
courses.description: This course examines nutritional and dietary requirements of actively exercising adults and those engaged in competitive sports. The course emphasizes the study of optimal nutritional regimens for improved fitness and performance, the role of supplements as ergogenic aids, and the integration of diet and exercise in achieving goal body weight and composition. Prerequisite: TI Physical Education 270<197>3 Units DS Fitness and Sports Nutrition AD Eligibility for English 1A AND proficient in pre-algebra AD skills

courses.title: Introduction to Criminology
courses.instructor: Mr. Jeffrey H. Inada, Mr. Christopher A. Johnson
courses.description: This course introduces students to the scientific analysis of crime and criminality. Sociological concepts and theories are used to analyze the nature, extent, and cause of crime; theoretical explanations for criminal behavior will be discussed. The dynamics of law, social control, treatment processes, and victimology will be evaluated. Note: This course is offered in the fall semester only. Prerequisite: TI Sociology 112<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Criminology AD Sociology 101 AND eligibility for English C1000

courses.title: Mass Communication
courses.instructor: Ms. Francesca C. Bishop, Larry R. Leach
courses.description: This course explores the history and effects of major forms of mass media in U.S. society, including television, radio, cinema, print, and new media. It encourages critical analysis of media messages and their cultural impact. The course appreciates works of philosophical, historical, literary, aesthetic, and cultural importance, recognizes diverse contributions to civilization, and promotes a humanizing perspective for holistic personal development. Prerequisite: TI Communication Studies 265<197>3 Units DS Mass Communication AD eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Communication Studies 5

courses.title: Sustainability in the Building Sector
courses.instructor: Mina R. Greas
courses.description: This launch course introduces students to the influence that the built-environment has in achieving sustainable outcomes that protect ecological systems, manage natural resources, and create healthy places. Students will learn about the various roles that the government, the advocates, and the building professions play in advancing such efforts. The course will include an exploration of the multiple educational and real-world career paths available. Prerequisite: TI Sustainabe Design 100<197>1 Unit DS Sustainability in the Building Sector AD Eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Stress Management/Healthy Living
courses.instructor: Ms. Danielle E. Roman, Mr. Gifford Lindheim, Andrew A. Alvillar, Robert C. Uphoff
courses.description: This course examines the effects of stress as it relates to work, sports, academics and daily life. Topics include understanding the nature of stress, the physiological and psychological reactions to stress and its effects on health (psychoneuroimmunology), assessments of personal coping styles, strategies for coping effectively with stress, relaxation techniques, mindfulness, and positive self-talk. Emphasis is placed on practical application of stress management techniques in daily life. Prerequisite: TI Physical Education 266<197>3 Units DS Stress Management for Healthy Living RP English 1A

courses.title: Music of the Beatles
courses.instructor: Dr. William E. Doyle
courses.description: This course surveys musical styles of the Beatles from 1957-1970. It focuses on the historical, aesthetic, socialpolitical, economic, and technological developments that have shaped the evolution of popular music. Emphasis is placed on identifying the Beatles' various musical periods, stylistic practices in compositions and concerts, and reflections of and impacts on the cultural and social issues of their time. Prerequisite: TI Music 117<197>3 Units DS Music of the Beatles AD Eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Music 17

courses.title: Introduction to Public Speaking
courses.instructor: Ms. Casey L. Jones, Dr. Julia Matthews, Julie A. Matos, Elyse Peterson, Ms. Francesca C. Bishop, Dr. Mark R. Crossman, Joseph D. Evans, Chantel Solomon, Ms. Brittany R. Hubble, Ms. Diana L. Crossman, Ms. Daryle D. Nagano, Valerie Ortega, Dr. Charmaine I. Kaimikaua, Lizette Alonso, Trisha Brodaksilva, Mr. Kelly Janke, Ms. Fariba Sadeghi-Tabrizi
courses.description: In this course, students learn and apply foundational rhetorical theories and techniques of public speaking in a multicultural democratic society. Students discover, develop, and critically analyze ideas in public discourse through research, reasoning, organization, composition, delivery to a live audience and evaluation of various types of speeches, including informative and persuasive speeches. Prerequisite: TI Communication Studies C1000<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Public Speaking AD eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Speech Communication 1, Communication Studies 1, Communication Studies 100

courses.title: Human Resources Management
courses.instructor: Mr. Yuen K. Chau
courses.description: This course is a study of effective utilization of human resources within an organization. Emphasis is placed on principles of human resources strategy and planning, recruitment, selection, training and development, employee appraisals, compensation and benefits, legal considerations, organized labor, and health and safety practices. Effective management of common issues in human resources from the perspective of the employee and employer are discussed. Prerequisite: TI Business 121<197>3 Units DS Human Resources Management AD Eligibility for English C1000 NT Formerly Business 21

courses.title: Political Philosophy
courses.instructor: Mr. Steven A. Gonzales, Dr. David L. Reed
courses.description: In this course students will examine political theory as presented in the primary works of major Western thinkers from Plato to Marx. Fundamental issues to be explored include human nature, justice, power, the role of the state, and the legitimate scope of government. Note: Political Science 7 is the same course as Philosophy 117. Prerequisite: TI Political Science 7<197>3 Units DS Political Philosophy AD Eligibility for English C1000 NT Political Science 7 is the same course as Philosophy 117.

courses.title: Oral Business Communications
courses.instructor: Mr. Lovell D. Alford
courses.description: In this course, students will compose and deliver original business presentations with focus on effective speaking in business situations. The course will focus on techniques of research, preparation, presentation, and evaluation. Various business formats including interviewing, delivering formal business presentations, speaking within a small group and interpersonal conversations, impromptu speaking and briefings will be covered. Note: Supervision 109 is the same course as Business 109. Prerequisite: TI Supervision 109<197>3 Units DS Oral Business Communications AD Eligibility for English C1000 or equivalent NT Formerly Supervision 29

courses.title: Business Office Administration
courses.instructor: Mr. Lovell D. Alford
courses.description: This course is designed to introduce business office administration, including the internal activities within the workplace. Topics include the workplace, business ethics, customer service, communications, workflow management, records management, and business ethics. In addition, students are introduced to critical professional skills such as leadership, working with teams, self-management, and personal career advancement. Note: This course is offered in the fall semester only. Prerequisite: TI Business 102<197>3 Units DS Business Office Administration AD Eligibility for English C1000 or equivalent NT Formerly Business 43

courses.title: U.S. History to 1877
courses.instructor: Hong Herrera Thomas, Saul J. Panski, Mr. Jamahl R. Melton, Wesley G. Willison, Mr. Muhammad Rafi, Juan C. Villalovos, Mr. Arne Jaaska, Mr. Mark T. Wishon, Ms. Sandra L. Uribe, Ms. Kirsten Resnick
courses.description: This course is a chronological survey of American history from the first Americans to 1877, focusing on American social, intellectual, political, economic, and diplomatic institutions. Major topics in the course include colonization, slavery, the American Revolution, Native Americans, the Civil War and Reconstruction. Note: The maximum UC credit allowed for students completing History 101 and 102 and History 110 and 111 and/or History 105 and 106 is one series. Prerequisite: TI History 101<197>3 Units DS United States History to 1877 AD eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Learning&development in College&career
courses.instructor: Hatien D. Nguyen, Ms. Amanda M. Colbeck, Ms. Sarah E. Leinen, Dr. Kristie L. Daniel-DiGregorio, Mr. Emmanuel Aguiar, Ms. Chanae' O. Wami, Ms. Alanah N. Liggins, Ms. Lauren K. Takahashi, Nikki L. Barber, Dr. Juli A. Soden
courses.description: This course provides an exploration of cognitive, psychological, social and physical factors influencing human behavior. Students will be introduced to psychological theories, research, and behavioral science principles used to understand individual behavior as it relates to achievement and fulfillment in higher education and careers. Topics include learning theory, higher education outcomes, critical thinking, motivation, self-efficacy, health and wellness, and interpersonal communication in a diverse world. Prerequisite: TI Human Development 110<197>3 Units DS Learning and Development in College and Career AD Eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Human Development 10

courses.title: Introduction to Psychology
courses.instructor: Mr. Eddie Galvan, Dr. Sharona Atkins, Ms. Jennifer L. Duarte Nagel, Ms. Arianna Yates, Mr. Micheal J. Huff, Mr. Julio G. Farias, III, Mr. Farshid Moshrefi, Ms. Jennifer A. Robinson, Lorrie Kato, Ms. Laris M. Rodriguez-Cintron, Dr. Amy J. Himsel, Ms. Sabine V. Huemer, Mr. Justin Huft, Daniel J. Pueblos, Ms. Kim-Lien T. Nguyen, Mr. Donte L. Green, Ms. Mary C. Stelter, Dr. Craig Sauer, Mr. Roberto Montes, Ms. Yara Farah, Dr. Renee Galbavy, Ms. Angel Hall, Ms. Jasmine M. Phillips, Ms. Stephanie Foley
courses.description: This course is an introduction to psychology, which is the study of the mind and behavior. Students focus on theories and concepts of biological, cognitive, developmental, environmental, social, and cultural influences; their applications; and their research foundations. This course is a survey study of human behavior and mental processes with an emphasis on basic theory and research generated by the scientific method. Prerequisite: TI Psychology C1000<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Psychology AD Eligiblity for English 1A or ENGL C1000 or equivalent NT formerly Psychology 5 and Psychology 101

courses.title: Human Sexuality
courses.instructor: Ms. Mary C. Stelter
courses.description: This course is the scientific study of human sexuality. Emphasis is placed on the analysis of sexuality from a psychological perspective. Topics include the history of human sexuality, the scientific research methods used to study sexuality, the psychobiology of sex, sexual behavior and relationships, and sex as a social and health issue. Gender and sociocultural issues are also addressed. Prerequisite: TI Psychology 112<197>3 Units DS Human Sexuality PR Psychology C1000 or Psychology C1000H with a minimum grade of PR C AD Eligibility for English C1000 NT formerly Psychology 12

courses.title: Entrprenurshp Start-Up Plning and Fundng
courses.instructor: Ms. Melissa Som de Cerff
courses.description: This course provides students with the ability to identify opportunities and develop them into viable small businesses or non-profit organizations. Students will focus on building the entrepreneurial skills needed to initiate and manage a small business or small organization. Areas of concentration include business planning, acquiring financing, drafting and pitching a business plan, marketing, accounting, legal forms of organizations, and strategic planning. Note: Letter grade or pass/no pass option. Prerequisite: TI Business 124<197>3 Units DS Entrepreneurship Start-up Planning and Funding AD Eligibility for English C1000 NT Formerly Business 24

courses.title: American Government and Politics
courses.instructor: Mr. Joshua J. Casper, Dr. Ellen L. Coleman, Angela M. Cranon-Charles, Eduardo E. Munoz, Mr. Van Chaney, Mr. Thomas Klein, Mr. Karl N. Striepe, Dr. Laurie Houske, Mr. Steven A. Gonzales, Dr. David L. Reed, Ms. Christine Jun
courses.description: This course is an introduction to government and politics in the United States and California. Students examine the constitutions, structure, and operation of governing institutions, civil liberties and civil rights, political behaviors, political issues, and public policy using political science theory and methodology. Prerequisite: TI Political Science C1000<197>3 Units DS American Government and Politics AD Eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Political Science 1 NT Students may take either Political Science C1000 or Political NT Science C1000H. Duplicate credit will not be awarded.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: TI Political Science C1000<197>3 Units DS American Government and Politics AD Eligibility for English 1A NT formerly Political Science 1 NT Students may take either Political Science C1000 or Political NT Science C1000H.

courses.title: Intro to Emergency Management
courses.instructor: Mr. Andrew N. Gulcher, Timothy Branley
courses.description: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of emergency management. Students will learn the principles of emergency management, and will become familiar with the major issues confronting emergency management providers. The course will emphasize the elements of the Incident Command System. Students will participate in a series of simulated emergency management exercises. Case studies of large scale emergency events will be covered. Prerequisite: TI Administration of Justice 21<197>3 Units DS Introduction to Emergency Management AD Administration of Justice 10 and eligibility for English 1A

courses.title: Music Appreciation Survey
courses.instructor: Dr. William E. Doyle, Dr. Kevin M. Blickfeldt, Dr. Patrick Schulz
courses.description: This course focuses on the major stylistic periods of Western art music from the Middle Ages until the end of the 20th Century. An emphasis will be placed on the music heard in North American and European concert halls. Examples will be presented in the context of contemporaneous social, political, and artistic movements and events. Basic musical elements, terminology, voice categories, and instruments of the orchestra will be included. Prerequisite: TI Music 111<197>3 Units DS Music Appreciation Survey AD Eligibility for English C1000 or equivalent NT formerly Music 11

courses.title: Computer Information Systems
courses.instructor: Dr. Junaid Siddiqui, Mr. Khai H. Lu, Mr. Hac H. Le, Ms. Kimberly S. Davis, Ms. Roni Allen, Mr. Martin Anaya, Mr. Richard A. Perkins
courses.description: This course introduces students to the concepts and technologies used in processing information in an organization. Topics include information systems, database management systems, networking, e-commerce, ethics and security, computer systems hardware, and applications. Students will apply these concepts and methods through hands-on projects developing computer-based solutions using application software. Prerequisite: TI Computer Information Systems 13<197>3 Units DS Computer Information Systems PR Eligibility for English 1A AD Business 115 or proficient in pre-algebra skills NT Office 365 will be used in all sections of Computer NT Information Systems 13.

courses.title: The Solar System
courses.instructor: Andrew D. Vanden Heuvel, Brooke E. Duitsman, Azul Celeste Rodriguez
courses.description: This course is an introduction to the major planets and the smaller members of the solar system such as moons, asteroids, Kuiper Belt Objects, and comets. Theories of the origin and histories of the planets are presented. The early history of astronomy and the Copernican Revolution are discussed. Sky phenomena such as the constellations, the seasons, eclipses, and planetary motions are demonstrated in the planetarium. The possibility of life elsewhere in the solar system is examined. Prerequisite: TI Astronomy 20<197>3 Units DS The Solar System AD English 84

courses.title: Financial Accounting
courses.instructor: Mr. Kurt Hull, Mr. Ridgway Knight, Ms. Ana Milosevic Nguyen, Ms. Julie Kim, Dr. Brizset Giles, Mr. Adam R. Margiotta, Mr. Sidney Porter
courses.description: This is an introductory course covering the fundamental theory, principles, and procedures of financial accounting with an emphasis on the corporate entity. Topics include ethics and the analysis, recording, and summarizing of accounting transactions on the accrual basis. Financial statements are prepared and analyzed. An emphasis will be on accounting as an information system to meet the demands of users for decision-making. Computer applications are integrated into the homework. Prerequisite: TI Business 150<197>4 Units DS Financial Accounting AD Eligibility for English C1000 or equivalent NT Formerly Business 1A

courses.title: Criminal Justice Career Preparation
courses.instructor: Oscar E. Serrano
courses.description: This course presents an overview of the criminal justice recruitment and selection process, and provides opportunities to gain knowledge and skills that will enable them to be more successful at passing the various testing phases in a criminal justice testing and background investigation process. Instruction begins with the application phase, then addresses the various steps in the hiring process including: the written examination, oral interview, written and oral psychological exams, polygraph exam, physical agility test and background investigation. Prerequisite: TI Administration of Justice 106<197>3 Units DS Criminal Justice Career Preparation AD Eligibility for English 1A
